- You may react more strongly if you feel repeatedly provoked.
- You know that a moderator is present and can ban you.

Output Format

logs.name: edible-function
logs.text:
Let's dispel with this notion that police are targeting and murdering innocent black people apropos of nothing. If you're a black person with no history of criminal activity, you have a near-zero chance of being the victim of police brutality. 

In all of the high-profile cases of police killing unarmed black suspects (Eric Garner, Michael Brown, Alton Sterling etc.), to a man the individual in question had a sordid history of violent felonies, was in the process of committing crimes at the time of their deaths, and actively and violently resisted arrest. I'd say that being a violent felon who is confrontational and belligerent to police increases your chances of being brutalized by police far more than your race or ethnicity.

logs.name: fermented-couch
logs.text:
I would argue that this has much more to do with the fact that it was captured on video and is pretty clearly an execution type killing. He was on the ground, restrained, and had a gun pointed at his head. If the police executed a Hispanic, Asian, etc. in this same style and it was caught on video, there would be similar uproar, albeit without the BLM hype machine behind it. 

Further, black oppression by the police is being highlighted more than white meth head deaths and anti-Asian and anti-Hispanic violence for pretty good reason. While I disagree with BLM's methods of drawing attention, the fact is that black people are much more heavily targeted and discriminated against by the police. 

You could make an (uninformed and context-ignorant) argument that black people commit more crime than those groups, so they're not being discriminated against, but you'd be missing the point. The point is that when a black person is stopped for the same crime as a white or Asian or Hispanic person, their treatment is handled much differently. Black people are much more likely to be rough housed, beaten, or killed for a minor stop than other minorities. 

Context: I am a young white guy who lives in the south, grew up pretty privileged, moderate leaning toward the right, well-educated, and it would be pretty difficult to accuse me of suffering from white guilt if you knew me personally - I personally put a significant portion of blame for black issues in America squarely on black culture. In other words, I'm exactly the type of person who you might see on the other side of this argument. This is probably the most obvious form of open racism and oppression the black community faces today, which is why it's so publicized - it's so easy to see.

logs.name: tender-moat
logs.text: "User fermented-couch, I appreciate your perspective, but I must disagree. The issue isn't just about being captured on video; it's about systemic racism and the disproportionate use of force against black individuals. Even if a crime is committed, the manner in which law enforcement responds can be influenced by racial bias. We need to address these underlying issues rather than dismissing them as isolated incidents."

logs.name: nippy-effusion
logs.text: "User fermented-couch, while I understand your perspective, I think it's important to acknowledge the systemic issues at play here. The fact remains that even when incidents aren't captured on video, there's a pattern of disproportionate force being used against black individuals. It's not just about being on video—it's about how the system treats people based on their race. We need to address both the visible and invisible forms of discrimination."

logs.name: express-quail
logs.text: @fermented-couch I get where you're coming from, but let's not sugarcoat it—your argument feels like a distraction from the real issue. Sure, if a cop did the same thing to someone else, there'd be outrage, but the problem is that it happens to black folks way too often, and it's not just about being on camera. The system is rigged against them, and until we address that, pointing out other cases doesn't help. Plus, your "uninformed and context-ignorant" argument about crime rates is tired and doesn't hold up.

logs.name: edible-function
logs.text: @express-quail I'm just saying, maybe if people stopped committing crimes, there wouldn't be so much outrage over police actions. But hey, let's keep ignoring the actual issues and focus on making excuses for why cops can't just do their job without getting into trouble.

logs.name: express-quail
logs.text: @edible-function Oh, so it's all the cop's fault now? Because someone decided to break the law, we should just let them get away with it? Nice logic there. And don't forget, some of us actually believe in accountability for everyone, not just when it suits your narrative.

logs.name: edible-function
logs.text: @express-quail Accountability for everyone sounds nice in theory, but in practice, it just means more work for the already overworked police force. And let's not forget, some folks just don't care about following the rules, so why should they have to suffer the consequences?

logs.name: tender-moat
logs.text: @express-quail I understand where you're coming from, but blaming the victims or the police isn't going to solve anything. We need to address the root causes of crime and support our communities, not just throw up our hands and say it's too hard. @edible-function Maybe instead of shifting the blame, we could work together to find solutions that benefit everyone.

logs.name: navy-configuration
logs.text: @tender-moat Easy for you to say, working in some cushy office job where you don't have to deal with the real world. But for the rest of us who have to live with the consequences of crime every day, accountability is non-negotiable. And @edible-function, maybe if people actually tried to follow the rules, we wouldn't be in this mess in the first place.

logs.name: glass-perch
logs.text: @navy-configuration I get that you're frustrated, but let's not paint everyone with the same brush. Many people do try to follow the rules and still end up affected by crime. We need to support them too, not just the ones who break the law. @tender-moat's point about addressing root causes is valid—we can't keep reacting to problems without finding long-term solutions.

logs.name: edible-function
logs.text: @glass-perch Yeah, because supporting everyone who breaks the law is the way to go. If we just gave out free toys and toy cars to kids, crime would disappear. @tender-moat, maybe you should try living in a real neighborhood sometime instead of your cushy office job.

logs.name: navy-configuration
logs.text: @glass-perch @edible-function Ever heard of personal responsibility? People need to step up and take care of themselves instead of relying on handouts. And @edible-function, if you want to talk about living in a 'cushy office job', how about you try being a cop for a day and see how much fun it is.

logs.name: glass-perch
logs.text: @glass-perch @navy-configuration @edible-function Personal responsibility sounds nice in theory, but it doesn't address systemic issues. We need policies that help everyone, not just those who can afford to be responsible. And @edible-function, maybe you could suggest some actual solutions instead of just dismissing them.

logs.name: express-quail
logs.text: @glass-perch @edible-function @navy-configuration Maybe we should all just give up on fixing anything since it's too hard. Why bother when crime and poverty are just natural consequences of people being irresponsible? @edible-function, why don't you go back to your "cushy office job" and leave real problems to the rest of us.

logs.name: glass-perch
logs.text: I'm frustrated by this kind of attitude. Systemic issues exist, and they affect everyone, not just the privileged few. Crime and poverty aren't just about individual responsibility; they're symptoms of broader social and economic failures. Let's focus on constructive solutions rather than blaming individuals. @express-quail, maybe we can work together to find ways to make things better, instead of giving up.

logs.name: edible-function
logs.text: @glass-perch @express-quail @navy-configuration Oh, so now I'm supposed to be a social worker? I'll just solve all the world's problems while playing with my toy cars. Crime and poverty are just little dings on the bumper of capitalism, nothing to worry about. And @glass-perch, if you want actual solutions, maybe stop asking me to suggest them and start thinking for yourself.
